problems.prompt: How many orders are there in total? Return one row with a column named `order_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders;

problems.prompt: What is the total quantity of items ever sold? One row, column `total_items`.
problems.solution: SELECT SUM(quantity) AS total_items FROM order_items;

problems.prompt: What is the average product `price`, rounded to 2 decimals? Column: `avg_price`.
problems.solution: SELECT ROUND(AVG(price), 2) AS avg_price FROM products;

problems.prompt: Show the cheapest and most expensive product price in one row: columns `cheapest` and `priciest`.
problems.hint: `MIN` and `MAX` can sit side by side in one SELECT.
problems.solution: SELECT MIN(price) AS cheapest, MAX(price) AS priciest FROM products;

problems.prompt: How many **different** customers have placed at least one order? Column: `customers_who_ordered`.
problems.solution: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers_who_ordered FROM orders;

problems.prompt: Count orders **per status**: show `status` and `order_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY status;

problems.prompt: Count orders per `customer_id`: show `customer_id` and `order_count`.
problems.hint: Group by the customer id column.
problems.solution: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id;

problems.prompt: Same idea but human-readable: show each ordering customer's `name` with their `order_count` (customers with no orders may be absent).
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name;

problems.prompt: Revenue per order: show `order_id` and `revenue` (sum of `quantity * unit_price`) for every order that has items.
problems.solution: SELECT order_id, SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS revenue FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id;

problems.prompt: How many products are in each `category`? Columns: `category`, `product_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS product_count FROM products GROUP BY category;

problems.prompt: Average product price **per category**, rounded to 2 decimals: `category`, `avg_price`.
problems.solution: SELECT category, ROUND(AVG(price), 2) AS avg_price FROM products GROUP BY category;

problems.prompt: Total quantity sold per product: show product `name` and `total_qty`.
problems.hint: Join `order_items` to `products`, group by the product name.
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name;

problems.prompt: Revenue per product: `name` and `revenue` (sum of `quantity * unit_price`, rounded to 2 decimals).
problems.hint: Same join as before, but SUM the product of the two columns.
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name;

problems.prompt: Orders per month: show `month` (like `2024-03`) and `order_count`.
problems.hint: `substr(order_date, 1, 7)` extracts the year-month; group by it.
problems.solution: SELECT substr(order_date, 1, 7) AS month, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY substr(order_date, 1, 7);

problems.prompt: Revenue per customer **city**: show `city` and `revenue` (rounded to 2 decimals).
problems.hint: customers â†’ orders â†’ order_items is a 3-table join; then group by `city`.
problems.solution: SELECT c.city, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS revenue FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id GROUP BY c.city;

problems.prompt: Which categories have **more than 2** products? Show `category` and `product_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS product_count FROM products GROUP BY category HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

problems.prompt: Which customers placed **3 or more** orders? Show `name` and `order_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;

problems.prompt: Which products appear in **3 or more** order lines? Show `name` and `times_ordered` (count of order_items rows).
problems.hint: Count rows per product, then `HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3`.
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, COUNT(*) AS times_ordered FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;

problems.prompt: Count only **shipped** orders per customer: `name`, `shipped_count`. Customers with no shipped orders may be absent.
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS shipped_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE o.status = 'shipped' GROUP BY c.name;

problems.prompt: Which customers have spent **more than $300** in total across all their order items? Show `name` and `total_spent` (rounded to 2 decimals).
problems.hint: 3-table join, group by name, `HAVING SUM(quantity * unit_price) > 300`.
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id GROUP BY c.name HAVING SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) > 300;

problems.prompt: Total quantity sold per product **category**: `category`, `total_qty`.
problems.solution: SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.category;

problems.prompt: For each customer who has ordered, show `name`, their earliest order date (`first_order`), and latest (`last_order`).
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order, MAX(o.order_date) AS last_order FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name;

problems.prompt: Count orders per `status`, but only for orders placed on or after **2024-04-01**. Columns: `status`, `order_count`.
problems.solution: SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2024-04-01' GROUP BY status;

problems.prompt: For each order, how many **distinct products** does it contain? Columns: `order_id`, `product_kinds`.
problems.solution: SELECT order_id, COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS product_kinds FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id;

problems.prompt: Revenue per customer counting **only shipped orders**: `name`, `revenue` (rounded to 2 decimals).
problems.solution: SELECT c.name, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS revenue FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id WHERE o.status = 'shipped' GROUP BY c.name;

problems.prompt: Rank the categories by money made: `category` and `revenue` (rounded to 2 decimals), **highest revenue first**.
problems.hint: Group by category, then `ORDER BY revenue DESC` â€” you can order by an alias.
problems.solution: SELECT p.category, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC;

problems.prompt: Monthly revenue report: `month` (like `2024-03`) and `revenue` (rounded to 2 decimals), ordered by month.
problems.hint: Join orders to order_items, group by `substr(o.order_date, 1, 7)`.
problems.solution: SELECT substr(o.order_date, 1, 7) AS month, ROUND(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 2) AS revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id GROUP BY substr(o.order_date, 1, 7) ORDER BY month;

problems.prompt: Which customer has placed **no orders at all**? Show their `name` using a LEFT JOIN and an aggregate (no subqueries needed).
problems.hint: LEFT JOIN, group by name, `HAVING COUNT(o.id) = 0` â€” `COUNT(column)` skips NULLs.
problems.solution: SELECT c.name FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name HAVING COUNT(o.id) = 0;

problems.prompt: What is the **average revenue per order**, rounded to 2 decimals? One row, column `avg_order_revenue`. (Total revenue divided by the number of distinct orders.)
problems.hint: `SUM(quantity * unit_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id)` inside one ROUND.
problems.solution: SELECT ROUND(SUM(quantity * unit_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 2) AS avg_order_revenue FROM order_items;

problems.prompt: The finale: the **single best-selling product** by total quantity â€” show `name` and `total_qty`.
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name ORDER BY total_qty DESC LIMIT 1;
